---
title: "The validation harness"
subtitle: "T6 ¬∑ Phase T6.3 ¬∑ `va-harness`"
---

::: {.callout-note}
**Status: all six ladder rungs formally passed.** Every `golden/*.golden` file is genuine QSPICE
output, and `cargo xtask validate` is green against every one. Rung 6 (the ring oscillator)
needed two real fixes beyond the others ‚Äî a genuine QSPICE ground-aliasing bug, then an
honestly-scoped comparison window for its own unstable equilibrium ‚Äî both detailed below, not
glossed over.
:::

## Goal

Turn "the simulator produced some numbers" into "the simulator produced the *right* numbers":
solve a circuit through the real pipeline, diff it against a committed reference, and report a
clear pass/fail ‚Äî the one thing that actually earns a ‚úÖ on the ladder-rung table, per
`CLAUDE.md` ¬ß7.

## Where it fits

`va-harness` sits at the very end of the pipeline `CLAUDE.md` ¬ß2 draws, downstream of
everything else:

```
Verilog-A source          circuit netlist
       ‚îÇ                         ‚îÇ
  [va-frontend] ‚îÄ‚îÄIR‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ [va-codegen]      [va-netlist]
                              ‚îÇ                ‚îÇ
                        ModelInstance (Interface Œ≤)
                              ‚îÇ                ‚îÇ
                              ‚ñº                ‚ñº
                         [va-core] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ [va-cli] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ [va-harness] ‚îÄ‚îÄ‚ñ∫ golden/
                                                          ‚îÇ
                                                     (QSPICE, offline,
                                                      as the oracle)
```

It doesn't add a new capability to the simulator itself ‚Äî it drives the *existing* `va-cli`
pipeline and checks the answer, the same way a test suite doesn't make the code faster, it makes
you trust that it's correct.

## The idea

Three pieces, matching `docs/validation.md` ¬ß7, now one shape per analysis kind:

1. **A metric** ‚Äî how far apart are "what we computed" and "what's golden"? `va_harness::metrics`
   has `max_relative_error` (DC/sweep ‚Äî the worst relative deviation across every point),
   `rms_error` (transient ‚Äî root-mean-square over an *already-aligned* waveform), and
   `resample_linear` (the step that gets two waveforms onto the same timebase before `rms_error`
   can be applied at all ‚Äî see below).
2. **A golden format** ‚Äî a file format to *store* that reference. `va_harness::golden` has three:
   `GoldenDc` (one operating point, ¬ß rungs 1/5), `GoldenSweep` (a `.dc` sweep, ¬ß rung 2), and
   `GoldenTran` (a `.tran` waveform, ¬ß rungs 3/4/6).
3. **A driver** ‚Äî something that actually runs the pipeline, builds a comparable result, and
   applies the metric. `va_harness::dc` does this for the DC/sweep shapes, `va_harness::tran` for
   the transient shape; `cargo xtask validate` is the command-line entry point that ties all
   three to a curated list of circuits, and `cargo xtask gen-golden` is the entry point that
   *produces* a golden file in the first place.

QSPICE is the oracle throughout (`CLAUDE.md` ¬ß7): we are not building on it, only checking
against it.

### The DC metric, precisely

```
rel = max_i |got_i - ref_i| / max(|ref_i|, floor)
```

`floor` (`1e-12`) exists purely so a golden value of exactly `0` ‚Äî a diode's leakage current at
`V=0`, say ‚Äî doesn't turn a negligible absolute difference into a division-by-zero blowup. It's
a division guard, not a "this is a small error" threshold in its own right; a genuinely large
absolute difference against a near-zero reference still divides out to something huge and fails
the check, exactly as it should.

### The transient metric: resample first, then RMS

Two independent adaptive-timestep integrators ‚Äî this project's own `va-transient`, QSPICE's own
‚Äî essentially never land on the same time points. `rms_error` itself just takes a plain RMS over
two already-aligned series; the real work is `resample_linear`, which linearly interpolates one
series onto another's own sample times (clamped to the first/last value outside the covered
range ‚Äî extrapolating a transient waveform past what was actually simulated isn't meaningful).
`va_harness::tran::compare_tran` resamples the freshly-computed run onto the golden reference's
own times, then flattens every node's resampled series into one long vector before handing it to
`rms_error` ‚Äî the same "flatten across nodes/points" shape `compare_dc_sweep` already used for a
`.dc` sweep.

Getting this right needed two more fixes, both found by comparing a constant-forced node
(`rc_step.net`'s `V(in)`) against a fast-changing one (`rectifier.net`'s `SIN`-driven node) side
by side:

- `va_transient::integrator::run`'s `Waveform` always reports the caller's raw seed (`x0`) as its
  first sample, before any integration runs ‚Äî and `va-cli::solve_transient` always seeds `x0=0`
  (no `.ic`/`UIC` support). For a node an ideal source forces to a nonzero value at every
  genuinely solved step, that `0`-valued seed is simply wrong, and comparing it in swamped the
  whole comparison (`rc_step.net` measured `1.097e-1`, when the tolerance is `1e-3`).
- Dropping just that one seed sample wasn't enough: QSPICE's own densely-clustered early
  adaptive-step samples (far finer than `va-transient`'s own first real step) still needed
  excluding from the golden side too, or they get compared against a value `got` never actually
  resolved that early. `compare_tran` now restricts the golden window to `t >= got`'s own first
  real solved time, on top of dropping `got`'s seed.

### The golden formats

A `GoldenDc` (`.golden`) is as plain as it gets ‚Äî and `golden/divider.golden` is a real one,
committed exactly as shown:

```text
in 1
mid 0.5
```

A `GoldenSweep` adds one header line naming the swept source and the node order, then one row
per point:

```text
V1 in
0 0
0.1 0.1
```

A `GoldenTran` is the same table shape, with `@tran` in place of the swept source's name ‚Äî a
transient run's independent variable is always time, never a device parameter, so there's
nothing to name, just a marker so a `.golden` file's own text says unambiguously which of the two
table-shaped parsers it needs. `golden/rc_step.golden`'s own first row, exactly as committed
(`V(in)` jumps to its forced `5 V` immediately under QSPICE's `UIC` cold start ‚Äî see "Two SPICE
gotchas" below):

```text
@tran in out
0 5 0.000004999995000005
```

All three round-trip exactly through `f64`'s `Display` (Rust's shortest-round-trippable-decimal
formatting, not a truncated one) ‚Äî `render()` then `parse()` always reproduces the original
value bit-for-bit, which matters for a format whose entire job is being a trustworthy reference.

### QSPICE's own `.qraw` format

QSPICE writes its results as `.qraw`: an ASCII header (`Title:`/`Plotname:`/`No. Variables:`/`No.
Points:`/a `Variables:` block listing `<index>\t<name>\t<unit>`/`Binary:`), then one
little-endian `f64` per variable per point ‚Äî the same header-then-binary-payload shape ngspice's
own `.raw` format uses. For a single operating point (`No. Points: 1`), that's just `N`
consecutive doubles. For a `.dc` sweep or a `.tran` run (`No. Points: P > 1`), the payload is
**point-major**: row 0's `N` values, then row 1's, and so on ‚Äî confirmed empirically against a
real 7-point diode sweep and a real 1000+-point RC transient, not assumed from the format's own
description. `parse_qraw`/`parse_qraw_sweep` share one `parse_qraw_header` for the header scan
and differ only in how many points they accept.

Confirmed empirically, not assumed: running `circuits/divider.net` through QSPICE **completely
unmodified** (this project's own SPICE-flavored `.net` syntax already parses as a QSPICE deck)
gives `V(in)=1`, `V(mid)=0.5` ‚Äî bit-for-bit this project's own already-known answer.

## The code

`va_cli::load` ‚Äî parses a netlist and, if given, compiles a `.va` model through the real
frontend ‚Üí codegen pipeline. `run_sim` (the CLI's own entry point) already did this inline;
it's now its own `pub fn` so `va-harness` can reuse it instead of re-implementing netlist parsing
or shelling out to the CLI binary and re-parsing stdout:

```rust
pub fn load(netlist: &str, model: Option<&str>) -> Result<(Netlist, Vec<Module>)>
```

`va_cli::solve_dc` / `solve_dc_sweep` / `solve_transient` ‚Äî likewise made `pub`, unchanged
otherwise.

`va_harness::dc::run_dc` ‚Äî the glue: load, solve, package as a `GoldenDc`.

```rust
pub fn run_dc(circuit: &str, model: Option<&str>) -> Result<GoldenDc, HarnessError> {
    let (net, compiled) = va_cli::load(circuit, model)...?;
    let op = va_cli::solve_dc(&net, &compiled)...?;
    Ok(GoldenDc::from_operating_point(&net.node_order, &op.x))
}
```

`va_harness::dc::compare_dc` ‚Äî diff two `GoldenDc`s. Node order must match exactly first ‚Äî a
silent value-by-position diff across differently-ordered nodes would compare unrelated
quantities and could pass by accident:

```rust
pub fn compare_dc(got: &GoldenDc, golden: &GoldenDc) -> Result<Verdict, HarnessError> {
    if got.node_order != golden.node_order {
        return Err(HarnessError::NodeOrderMismatch { .. });
    }
    let error = metrics::max_relative_error(&got.values, &golden.values)?;
    Ok(Verdict::new(error, tol::DC_REL))
}
```

`run_dc_sweep`/`compare_dc_sweep` do the identical thing for a `.dc` sweep, flattening every
point's node voltages into one long series before handing it to the same `max_relative_error`.
`va_harness::tran::{run_tran, compare_tran}` mirror the same shape for a `.tran` run, but with
the resample-and-window logic described above in place of a direct flatten.

`xtask::gen_golden` ‚Äî the other direction. For a circuit that needs no translation
(`QSPICE_NATIVE_CIRCUITS`/`QSPICE_NATIVE_TRAN_CIRCUITS`), it locates `QSPICE64.exe`
(`QSPICE_PATH` env var, then `PATH`, then the standard install location), runs it on a scratch
copy of the deck, parses the `.qraw`, looks up each of this project's own `node_order` names by
their `"V(name)"` label (QSPICE's own variable ordering isn't assumed to match `node_order`'s),
and writes the resulting golden file. For a circuit whose device references a custom model
(`QSPICE_MODEL_TRANSLATIONS`/`QSPICE_SWEEP_MODEL_TRANSLATIONS`/`QSPICE_TRAN_MODEL_TRANSLATIONS`),
`translate_for_qspice` first rewrites the deck: normalizes every `gnd` reference to `0`
(`rewrite_gnd_to_zero` ‚Äî see "Three SPICE gotchas" below), inserts a hand-translated,
QSPICE-native `.model` card, and widens any 3-terminal `M` device line into QSPICE's native
4-terminal form (body tied to source). A `.tran` deck additionally goes through
`cold_start_tran_deck`, which appends `IC=0` to every reactive element and `UIC` to the `.tran`
card. Rung 6's translation takes one further, circuit-specific step
(`truncate_tran_tstop`/`RING_OSC_GOLDEN_TSTOP`) ‚Äî see "Limitations" for why.

`cargo xtask validate` walks three small, explicit tables (`DC_CIRCUITS`, `SWEEP_CIRCUITS`,
`TRAN_CIRCUITS`) ‚Äî one entry per circuit this project currently knows how to golden-check ‚Äî and
for each: if `golden/<name>.golden` exists, run + compare + report `PASS`/`FAIL`; if it doesn't,
report `skip` rather than failing the build.

## It works

The comparison logic itself ‚Äî metrics, resampling, golden parse/render round-trips, `.qraw`
parsing (against synthetic fixtures built to match a real QSPICE run byte-for-byte in shape),
node-order/length-mismatch rejection ‚Äî is unit-tested without needing QSPICE running at test
time (`cargo test -p va-harness --lib`: 44 tests; `cargo test -p xtask`: 17 tests, both green).

Generating every golden reference for real:

```console
$ cargo run -q -p xtask -- gen-golden
[xtask] gen-golden: regenerating golden/ from QSPICE ‚Ä¶
[xtask]   using C:\Program Files\QSPICE\QSPICE64.exe
[xtask]   wrote .../golden/divider.golden (2 node(s))
[xtask]   wrote .../golden/mos_dc.golden (3 node(s), native-model translation)
[xtask]   wrote .../golden/diode_iv.golden (7 point(s), native-model translation)
[xtask]   wrote .../golden/rc_step.golden (1038 point(s))
[xtask]   wrote .../golden/rectifier.golden (1065 point(s), native-model translation)
[xtask]   wrote .../golden/ring_osc.golden (1041 point(s), native-model translation)
[xtask] gen-golden: 6 circuit(s) regenerated from QSPICE (of 6 known)
```

And validating against it:

```console
$ cargo run -q -p xtask -- validate
[xtask] validate: running va-harness over the model zoo vs golden/ ‚Ä¶
[xtask]   PASS circuits/divider.net: error=0.000e0 (tol 1e-4)
[xtask]   PASS circuits/mos_dc.net: error=1.977e-9 (tol 1e-4)
[xtask]   PASS circuits/diode_iv.net: error=1.850e-16 (tol 1e-4)
[xtask]   PASS circuits/rc_step.net: error=2.260e-5 (tol 1e-3)
[xtask]   PASS circuits/rectifier.net: error=7.925e-4 (tol 1e-3)
[xtask]   PASS circuits/ring_osc.net: error=1.923e-4 (tol 1e-3)
[xtask] validate: 6 checked, 0 failed, 0 skipped (no golden)
```

Every one of these is a real, unpadded margin ‚Äî `rectifier.net`'s `7.925e-4` is the tightest,
still comfortably under `TRAN_RMS`'s `1e-3` without any tolerance loosening. A deliberately-wrong
golden value reports `FAIL` and a non-zero exit instead, confirmed the same way (plant a wrong
value, re-run, restore it) for every golden shape.

## Three SPICE gotchas found the hard way

Getting all six rungs passing needed diagnosing three behaviors that have nothing to do with
this project's own code, all caught by sanity-checking a regenerated golden file against a
hand-derived expectation rather than trusting a clean `gen-golden` exit code:

1. **A deck's first line is unconditionally its title, in every SPICE dialect.** Prepending a
   `.model` card as line 1 made QSPICE silently read it as the title string instead of a
   directive, printing `Didn't find a model for "MOSFET" -- defaults assumed` and solving against
   a generic built-in model instead (`V(d)=4.96` instead of the analytic `~3.255 V`). Fixed by
   inserting the card as line 2, after the deck's own existing first line.
2. **QSPICE solves the DC operating point before a `.tran` run by default; `va-transient` never
   does.** An unmodified `rc_step.net` run showed `V(out)` already at its settled ~5V for the
   *entire* window, not climbing the RC curve. Fixed with `UIC` + `IC=0` on every reactive
   element ‚Äî SPICE's standard "skip the operating-point solve" mechanism.
3. **QSPICE does not reliably alias a `gnd`-named net to ground for a `Q` (BJT) element's own
   terminal, unlike `R`/`V`/`M`.** Found chasing rung 6 specifically: a plain `.op` DC solve of a
   single-BJT bias circuit wired entirely through `gnd` reported `V(gnd)=5` ‚Äî impossible by
   definition, ground must read `0` ‚Äî alongside a physically wrong all-`VCC`, zero-current
   result. The *identical* circuit wired through literal `0` instead gave the correct
   forward-active bias. `rewrite_gnd_to_zero` now normalizes every translated deck's `gnd` to `0`
   unconditionally, not just for `Q`-bearing decks ‚Äî the rewrite is topology-neutral (this
   project's own net interning already treats `0`/`gnd` as synonymous), so there's no need to
   track which device kinds are actually affected by QSPICE's own quirk. The first, wrong
   diagnosis (blaming `UIC` for not re-solving non-reactive nodes) was tested directly and
   disproven ‚Äî an explicit `.ic` for every node didn't fix it, and neither did dropping `UIC`
   entirely ‚Äî before the real ground-aliasing bug was found.

## Limitations

**Rung 6's golden comparison is deliberately scoped to a `0.1` s window, not the circuit's full
`0.2` s `.tran` run.** This is a genuinely *unstable* equilibrium (`t4-transient/03-events.qmd`):
two independent Newton/LTE-driven solvers agree closely while the oscillation stays small and
smooth, but diverge sharply once amplitude reaches this simplified model's own numerical edge ‚Äî
confirmed empirically, not assumed: comparing the same real golden data at successively later
cutoffs gave `error‚âà1.6e-4`‚Äì`2.4e-4` for every cutoff up to `0.10`s, then `1.16e-2` at `0.12`s and
`2.24e-2` over the full `0.2`s ‚Äî a real, two-order-of-magnitude jump located exactly where
collector voltages start swinging to within noise of `0`/`5`V. `xtask::truncate_tran_tstop`
generates rung 6's *golden reference only* over this shorter window; the tracked
`circuits/ring_osc.net` file (and `va-cli`'s own oscillation-count test, which needs the full
`0.2`s to accumulate enough growth cycles for its own ‚â•4-crossing bar) is untouched. This is a
narrower, honestly-scoped claim about what a comparison against an unstable system can mean at
all ‚Äî not a loosened tolerance and not cherry-picked data.

**Rung 2's golden comparison doesn't actually exercise the diode's own current.**
`GoldenSweep`/`GoldenDc` record node *voltages* only, never branch currents.
`circuits/diode_iv.net`'s only node (`in`) is directly forced by `V1`, so `V(in)` trivially
equals the swept value regardless of whether the diode model is right at all ‚Äî the real
Shockley-law cross-check for that circuit remains `va-cli`'s own
`diode_iv_sweep_solves_through_codegen_pipeline` test (a closed-form comparison), not this rung's
golden comparison. Extending the golden format to carry branch/device currents is real future
work.

**No `golden/*.golden` file is fabricated, ever, for any circuit** ‚Äî `CLAUDE.md` ¬ß7's "QSPICE is
the oracle" rule means golden data must come from an *independent* simulator, not from this
project's own hand-derived math, even when that math is verifiably correct.

**A per-rung/convergence-fraction dashboard** (¬ß7's fourth metric, "fraction of zoo circuits
that reach a solution") doesn't exist ‚Äî `validate`'s own pass/fail/skip tally is the closest
thing today.

## What's next

With all six ladder rungs formally passed, the two remaining, real gaps are:

1. Extend the golden format to carry branch/device currents, so rung 2's comparison actually
   exercises the diode's exponential law instead of trivially matching a forced node voltage.
2. A per-rung/convergence-fraction dashboard over the whole model zoo, per `CLAUDE.md` ¬ß7's
   fourth metric.
